div.head: CO–39. Memorandum from the Under Secretary of State for Political Affairs (Merchant) to the PresidentSource: Department of State, Central Files, 721.5-MSP/12–3160. Secret. Drafted by Joseph B. Kyle of the Office of the Deputy Coordinator for Mutual Security.

list.head: SUBJECT
list.item: Determinations under Sections 105(b)(4) and 451(a) of the Mutual Security Act of 1954,For text of the Act (Public Law 665), approved August 26, 1954, see 68 State 832. as amended, permitting the furnishing of military assistance to Colombia

div.p: The purpose of this memorandum is to recommend that you make the requisite determinations under Sections 105(b)(4) and 451(a) of the Mutual Security Act of 1954, as amended (hereinafter referred to as “the Act”), to permit the furnishing of military assistance to Colombia.

The proposed military assistance is needed primarily to enable the Colombian Government to expand its campaign against organized bandits and guerrillas. For the past decade, these bandits and guerrillas have caused an appalling loss of life and destruction of property, and the capabilities of the Colombian Government to maintain internal security and to resist external aggression progressively have been weakened. Communist influence reportedly is increasing in the areas infested by bandits, and agents of the Castro Government in Cuba are known to be active among anti-government groups. In these circumstances, it is considered to be important to the security of the United States to promote stability in the Caribbean area by furnishing military assistance to Colombia for its internal security upon a determination under Section 105(b)(4) of the Act that internal security requirements may be the basis of this program and, pursuant to Section 451(a) of the Act, without regard to the requirement of the first sentence of Section 105(b)(4) of
the Act that such assistance in the form of equipment and materials be furnished only in furtherance of western Hemisphere defense missions.

Enclosed is a detailed statement of the background and justification of this program, the implementation of which
is estimated to require the expenditure of up to $1,750,000 of funds available under the Act for military assistance, of which up to $1,500,000 may be used to furnish equipment and materials. A small amount of excess equipment and materials may be included.

It is considered that the furnishing of the proposed assistance in the form of equipment and materials and the use of funds available thereafter under the Act are in furtherance of the purposes of the Act and are important to the security of the United States.

IT IS, ACCORDINGLY, RECOMMENDED that you sign the enclosed memorandum which contains the determinations required by the Act in order to carry out such military assistance to Colombia.The referenced memorandum containing the necessary Presidential Determinations, designated No. 61-14, recommended by Dillon and conurred in by John N. Irwin, II, Assistant Secretary of Defense for International Security Affairs, on Decenber 21, and Maurice H. Stans, Director of the Bureau of the Budget, on December 29, is not printed.The following typed note appears on the source text: “Approved by the White House; see Memo to the Secretary of State dated 1/5/61 from the President.” The note is initialed, but the initials are indecipherable.

frus:attachment.head: Military Assistance for Colombia Requiring Determinations, Under the Mutual Security Act of 1954, as amended
frus:attachment.p: The assistance proposed for Colombia includes initial equipment and related services and specialized training for the Colombian Military establishment at an estimated total cost of $1,750,000. Equipment to be furnished would include vehicles, communications equipment, and air transport equipment. It is expected that the equipment and materials portion of the proposed assistance will cost up to $1,500,000. There will be a continuing modest annual requirement for maintenance and spares, but it is expected that the Colombian Government will be able to finance this continuing cost itself. A small amount of the initial equipment and materials may come from excess stocks.

Training, at an estimated cost of $250,000, would be provided by United States training advisors or at selected United States installations in such subjects as counter-guerrilla warfare, intelligence and
counter-intelligence, civil affairs, psychological warfare, public and troop information, and criminal investigation.

In 1948, following the assassination of Jorge Eliécer Caitán, leader of the Liberal Party, the Liberals attempted to overthrow the Conservative government. Failing in this effort, Liberal leaders and many of their followers fled to the almost inaccessible rural areas of Colombia. They subsequently were joined by other Liberals persecuted by the Conservatives in that party’s attempt to extirpate the Liberals as a major political force, by criminal elements desirous of reaping personal advantage from the political chaos attending the civil warfare that ensued, and by subversive elements interested in promoting the cause of international Communism. Well organized and well equipped gangs of bandits and guerrillas preyed upon isolated peasants and small villages, disrupted communications facilities, and laid waste extensive areas of Colombia’s agricultural land. Between 1948 and 1958, it is estimated that over 200,000 persons lost their lives as a direct result of banditry and guerrilla warfare and property damage totaled hundreds of millions of dollars.

In 1956, with Colombia’s status as a viable political entity in doubt, Liberal and Conservative leaders agreed to merge their differences in the interests of national
unity. President Gustavo Rojas Pinilla was deposed in May 1957. Following an interim government presided over by a military junta, the Liberals-Conservative coalition implemented an unusual—although so far workable—formula for governing the nation. Under the guidance of Alberto Lleras Camargo, now President of Colombia, the Liberals and Conservatives agreed that for a period of 16 years they would collaborate in a bi-partisan parity government called the National Front.

The Liberal-Conservative rapprochement has not meant an end of organized banditry or guerrilla activities. Gangs of bandits and guerrillas still roam throughout much of rural Colombia. In five provinces martial law is still in effect. In recent months, bandit activity has spread to three provinces hitherto untouched. Most of the gangs are comprised of criminal elements for whom banditry has become a way of life. There is increasing evidence, however, that Communist and pro-Castro elements have penetrated many of the gangs and, in some cases, the gangs are acting under direct Communist leadership.

The Colombian armed forces have been unable to eradicate the bandits. They have devoted their main effort to restricting them to the mountainous regions in which they are strongest. While incidents of violence have diminished considerably during the two years that Lleras Camargo has been President, the problem actually has grown more acute. The fear of residents of bandit-infested areas that they will be killed if they cooperate with the forces of law and order restrains a large segment of the population from cooperating with the government
in its efforts to solve the problem. Refugees from bandit-infested rural areas have flocked to the cities where they create a serious social problem. They are easy prey for Communist and pro-Castro agitators. Lleras Camargo is of the opinion, which is supported by United States Government representatives in Colombia, that his government’s efforts to resolve some of Colombia’s basic social and economic problems within a democratic framework are doomed to failure unless the bandit problem is resolved.

The Colombian military establishment has excellent personal. Its professionals have excellent training in military theory but limited practical training in field operations, particularly counter-guerrilla activities. It lacks field equipment, both individual and organization, and it is short of transportation and communication equipment. In June 1959
President Lleras Camargo asked the United States to consider assisting him in attacking the bandit and guerrilla problem. In November-December 1959 this government sent to Colombia a team of anti-guerrilla warfare experts to survey the problem. The team’s recommendations were mainly for steps the Colombians themselves could take, with United States technical assistance, e.g., to develop specially trained anti-guerrilla forces, to establish effective intelligence and information services, and to undertake a program to install confidence in the armed forces. The team also recommended that special material be provided to enable the Colombian armed forces to combat the guerrillas in the field. The team’s recommendations have been accepted in principle by the responsible United States Government representatives in Colombia.

Upon receipt of assurance from the Colombian Government that it is in agreement with certain courses of action which are to be submitted to it for consideration by the United States Government, and on receipt of a formal request for United States assistance, the Department of State and the Department of Defense recommend that United States be prepared to offer grant military assistance in the form of equipment, materials, related services, and training.

It is considered to be in the United States interest to provide Colombia with the military equipment, materials, and services described above using up to $1,500,000 of funds available for military assistance in fiscal year 1961. The furnishing of military assistance on a grant basis to any Latin American nation is subject to several conditions. First, under Section 105(a) and 143 of the Act, the President must determine that the increased ability of the nation to defend itself is important to the security of the United States, and that the furnishing of such assistance will strengthen the security of the United States and promote world peace. Second, Section 105(b)(4) of the Act provides that military equipment and materials may be furnished to a Latin American nation only in furtherance of missions directly relating to the common defense of the western hemisphere and that, unless the President determines
otherwise, internal security requirements shall not be the basis for military assistance programs to American Republics. Third, pursuant to the Sections 141 and 142(a) of the Act, prior to the furnishing of equipment or materials, the recipient nation must have agreed to the undertakings which are specified by Section 142(a) of the Act and normally included in the conventional
military assistance agreement. Fourth, under Section 511(c) of the Act, any nation receiving military equipment or materials on a grant basis must make arrangements for the return to the United States of any such equipment and materials when no longer required for the purposes for which furnished.

The first condition has been satisfied. In the President’s message on the Mutual Security program of February 19. 1958,For text, see Public Papers of the Presidents: Dwight D. Eisenhower, 1958 (Washington: Government Printing Office, 1959), pp. 160–168. the necessary findings were confirmed as to the countries for which military assistance was then proposed or had been furnished, Colombia being among such countries.

The second condition requires a Presidential determination under Section 105(b)(4) and 451(a) of the Act to permit the proposed program. The military assistance proposed to be furnished would be without reference assistance to Western Hemisphere defense missions and would strengthen the internal security capability of the Colombian armed forces and be used for internal security purposes. Therefore, it is considered necessary as to the equipment and the materials element of the proposed assistance only to request that the President waive the first of the two above-mentioned requirements of Section 105(b)(4) of the Act and make the determination required by the third sentence of Section 105(b)(4). The first sentence of Section 105(b)(4) is not applicable to assistance in the form of training or other services, and, as to training, the function of making the determination required by the third sentence of Section 105(b)(4) has been delegated to the Secretary of State.

The third and fourth conditions have been satisfied also by a military assistance agreement effected by an exchange of notes on April 17, 1952.For text of the agreement, see 3 UST (pt. 3) 3690. However, this military assistance agreement does not apply to the assistance herein proposed, namely, the furnishing of military equipment, materials and services to assist Colombia to maintain its internal security without reference to Western hemisphere defense missions. Therefore, a further agreement will be sought with Colombia to satisfy the conditions required by Sections 141 (second sentence), 142(a) and 511(c) of the Act with respect to the assistance program herein proposed.
